{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Owari-zukuri]] {{Gion cult}}

The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Owari-zukuri]] {{Gion cult}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Owari-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia3]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia3]] {{Gion cult}}

It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Owari-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] {{Gion cult}}

The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Owari-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]]{{Gion cult}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Owari-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia24]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]]{{Gion cult}}

It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Owari-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia24]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]]{{Gion cult}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Owari-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia24]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]]{{Gion cult}}

Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Owari-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]]{{Gion cult}}

The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Owari-zukuri]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Images]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]]{{Gion cult}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:kk]][[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[category:kk]][[category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Gion shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[category:kk]][[category:Owari-zukuri]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Images]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]]{{Gion cult}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:kk]][[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[category:kk]][[category:Owari-zukuri]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Shrine]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsushima Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[category:Tsushima, Aichi]] [[category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[category:Gion shrines]] [[category:Important Cultural Properties of Aichi Prefecture]] [[category:Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[category:Owari-zukuri]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsushima Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsushima Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tsushima Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]]{{Gion cult}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, 津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Tsushima Shrine]] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] designated tangible cultural property]] [[category:Owari-zukuri]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Tsushima Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from Wikipedia]] The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, [[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from Wikipedia]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Tsushima Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water.
