revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Expanded pages from Wikipedia
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons Category|Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)|Tsushima Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Gion cult}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|津島神社|version=101876956|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[sn:Tsushima Shrine]][[Category:Tsushima Shrines]] [[sn:Tsushima Shrine]][[sn:Tsushima Shrine]]}} [[sn:Tsushima Shrine]] [[en:Tsushima Shrine]] [[fr:Tsushima-jinja]] revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia 4
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{commons revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[:en:Owari Tokugawa clan|Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[:en:Owari Tokugawa clan|Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[:en:Owari Tokugawa clan|Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[:en:Owari Tokugawa clan|Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} 津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q705136]] Wikidata name: Tsushima Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:32, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[:en:Owari Tokugawa clan|Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} 津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[:en:Owari Tokugawa clan|Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q705136]] Wikidata name: Tsushima Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:32, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[:en:Owari Tokugawa clan|Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[:en:Owari Tokugawa clan|Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} [[sn:commons:Category:Tsushima-jinja (Tsushima, Aichi)]][[en:Tsushima Shrine]][[fa:معبد تسوشیما]][[fr:Tsushima-jinja]][[ja:津島神社]][[zh:津島神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q705136]] Wikidata name: Tsushima Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:32, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tsushima, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tsushima Shrine
津島神社 | image = Tsushimajinja4.JPG | caption = The [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']] of Tsushima Shrine | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|10|42|N|136|43|07|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Tsushima Shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]/[[Gion|Gozutennō]] | founded_by = | established = 540 AD | date_destroyed = | location = Tsushima, Aichi | website = {{URL|http://tsushimajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tsushima Shrine'''|津島神社|Tsushima Jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]], Japan. It is the head shrine of a nationwide [[Shinto shrine#Shrine networks|shrine network]] of shrines dedicated to the {{nihongo|Tsushima Cult|津島信仰|''Tsushima Shinkō''}}, Centered primarily in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]], this network has approximately 3,000 shrines and is the tenth-largest network in the country. The main ''[[kami]]'' of this faith are {{nihongo|[[Gozu Tennō]]|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|lit. ox-headed heaven king}}, the god of pestilences, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], two deities that have been conflated together.{{EOS|Gozu Tennō|192|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} For this reason, like other shrines of the network it is also called {{nihongo|Tsushima Gozutennō-sha|津島牛頭天王社|lit. Tsushima Gozutennō Shrine}}.{{EOS|Gion/Tsushima Shinkō|1075|Yonei, Teruyoshi|2-5-2011}} See [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] for more info. ==History== Shrine legend, unsupported by any historical documentation, claims that the shrine was founded in [[:en:Tsushima, Aichi|Tsushima]] by the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] (343-215 BCE) to worship Gozutennō's ''[[:en:Mitama|aramitama]]'' (its violent side), which remained at [[Izumo-taisha]], and it's ''[[:en:Mitama|nigemitama]] (calm aspect)'' which came to Japan from the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]] after stopping in [[Tsushima Island]], between Korea and Japan. The shrine relocated to its current location in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] in 540 CE. This may explain the relationship between the two Tsushimas suggested by the common name. The shrine appears in historical records from the time of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] (786-846 CE), during whose Rank it was awarded the status of First Court Rank, indicating that it was of considerable importance and antiquity by that time. It was awarded the title of ''Tennō-sha'' by [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] (980-1011 CE); however, for unknown reasons, it is not mentioned at all in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records completed in 927 CE, nor in the official records of the province. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] built Shobata Castle in the vicinity of the shrine, and the [[Mon (emblem)|family crest]] of the Oda clan is the same emblem as that used by the Tsushima Shrine, indicating a close connection. The shrine was subsequently repaired by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], and it received official status and patronage by the [[:en:Owari Tokugawa clan|Owari Tokugawa clan]] of [[Owari Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. With the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], Tsushima Shine was initially Ranked as a prefectural shrine in [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], and its status was increased to that of a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or National Shrine, 3rd Rank, in 1926. In 1920, the ''[[Honden]]'' of the shrine, which was built in 1605 under the patronage of [[Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], was designated an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. The building is built in the [[Shinto architecture#Owari-zukuri|owari-zukuri]] style, of which few examples remain.{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/174472 |title=津島神社本殿 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'' gate, built in 1591, was also designated an Important Cultural Property in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/189073 |title=津島神社楼門 |language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }} The shrine holds a festival called {{nihongo|Tsushima Matsuri|津島祭り}} in the sixth month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (July in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]) during which boats called {{nihongo|''danjiri''|車楽}} are floated on the Tennō River, and reeds are released into the water. ==Gallery== File:Tsushimajinja3.JPG|Rōmon File:Tsushimatennosai1.JPG|[[:en:Tenno Matsuri|Owari Tsushima Tennōsai(eve)]] ==References== {{Reflist}}
